User Input & Data Conversion

Moving from chaos to control in TypeScript.

Agenda

  • Project Initialization
  • ESM vs CommonJS
  • Capturing Input
  • Data Conversion
  • Input Validation
  • Output Formatting

Project Initialization

  • Install TypeScript as devDependency
  • Ensure version consistency
  • Avoid global installations
npm install --save-dev typescript
Always install TypeScript locally to the project to avoid version conflicts between different applications.

Node.js Type Definitions

  • TypeScript needs Node metadata
  • Install @types/node package
  • Prevents module resolution errors
npm install --save-dev @types/node
Without these types, TypeScript won't recognize native modules like 'readline' or 'process'.

ESM vs CommonJS

  • Modern Node uses ES Modules
  • Set "type": "module"
  • Enables "import" syntax
{
  "type": "module"
}
Modifying package.json is critical for using modern import/export syntax in Node.js.

The Input Workflow

  • 1. Capture raw data
  • 2. Convert to types
  • 3. Validate the data
  • 4. Format for display
This four-phase pipeline ensures we treat user input as the 'Wild West' and tame it before use.

Capturing Input

  • Use node:readline/promises module
  • Awaits user response
  • Pauses script execution
import * as readline from 'node:readline/promises';
const rl = readline.createInterface({ 
  input: process.stdin, 
  output: process.stdout 
});
The promises-based API allows for cleaner code using async/await patterns.

Phase 1: Capture

  • All input starts as string
  • Even numbers are strings
  • Use await rl.question()
const rawInput = await rl.question('Price: ');
Never assume the type of input; the terminal always provides a string initially.

Phase 2: Conversion

  • Transform strings to numbers
  • Use parseFloat() for decimals
  • Prevents logic errors
const price: number = parseFloat(rawInput);
Without conversion, '5' + '5' becomes '55' instead of 10.

The ParseFloat Gotcha

  • Partial parsing can occur
  • '55abc' might become 55
  • Always follow with validation
ParseFloat is helpful but not foolproof; it stops at the first non-numeric character.

Phase 3: Validation

  • Input is the 'Wild West'
  • Use isNaN() to check
  • Reject bad data early
if (isNaN(price)) {
  console.log('Invalid number!');
  rl.close();
  process.exit();
}
This is 'defensive programming'—stopping the app before corrupted data causes a crash.

Phase 4: Formatting

  • Polish data for UI
  • Use toFixed(2) for currency
  • Converts number back to string
const display = price.toFixed(2);
console.log(`Total: $${display}`);
Formatting is the final step to make raw numeric data user-friendly.

Closing the Interface

  • Call rl.close() when finished
  • Prevents terminal hanging
  • Returns control to prompt
Forgetting to close the readline interface is a common 'gotcha' that keeps the process running indefinitely.

TypeScript Best Practices

  • Explicitly declare your types
  • Avoid the 'any' type
  • Leverage IDE autocomplete
let count: number = 10; // Good
let data: any = "hi"; // Bad
Using 'any' defeats the purpose of TypeScript. Be explicit to catch bugs early.

Defensive Programming

  • Never trust external data
  • Validate at the boundary
  • Ensure environment is typed
Treat every piece of user input as potentially malicious or malformed.

Full Workflow Example

const raw = await rl.question('Enter: ');
const num = parseFloat(raw);
if (isNaN(num)) return rl.close();
console.log(num.toFixed(2));
rl.close();
This snippet combines all four phases: Capture, Convert, Validate, and Format.

Summary

  • Use ESM and @types/node
  • Capture strings with Readline
  • Convert and validate immediately
  • Format output for users
  • Never trust user input
